summary: Asparouhova examines the puzzling Pew Research finding that 40% of millennials support government censorship of speech offensive to minorities, despite being the most tolerant generation on social issues. She argues this apparent paradox stems from how social media has transformed speech itself—we share outrage-inducing content to strengthen our identities, and collective online shaming has become a form of peer-to-peer censorship. The essay suggests that while individually exercising free speech, we collectively create censorious mob dynamics that ruin lives. She concludes that we've achieved a dystopian version of world peace: not diverse tolerance, but citizens policing adherence to politically correct views.
key_points: 40% of millennials supporting government censorship seems paradoxical given their high tolerance for diversity and progressive social views
Increased access to information online has counterintuitively made people want to censor more, not less
Social media outrage-sharing strengthens personal identity but contributes to collective censorship through mob dynamics
Censorship is no longer primarily a government function—it happens through social media pile-ons and public shaming
Edge cases where speech is most offensive are precisely where free speech protections matter most
money_quotes: It seems counterintuitive that increased access to information online should make us want to censor more, not less.
These are the edge cases where the protection of free speech matters most.
The content that pisses us off the most is also the content we share the most, because it strengthens our identity.
We all face a choice: keep sharing that content and getting mad about it, or recognize the futile masturbatory nature of our acts and let it go.
These days, with enough angry retweets, anyone can censor anything.
Writing an angry post about so-and-so's latest offensive remark is technically an exercise of your right to free speech, but as part of a critical mass, also contributes to censorship of your peers.

World peace is here, but it's a little dystopian. Rather than the colorful patchwork quilt envisioned by 1960s free-love hippies, we've got citizens policing adherence to a politically correct set of views.
key_insight: Social media has democratized censorship—individual acts of free speech aggregate into collective mob dynamics that suppress dissent more effectively than government ever could.
